name: adversarial/bracket-bedlam
html:
wiki and deeper n and cut short xbar)

[x](a b) never links, and [unclosed](drifts across the newline without linking

[alt with ] bracket] and [**alt is plain text**]

[/orphan] first, then a[/marquee] mismatch survives

classic but [color=]is literal and quoted

:smile: but not :no space:, not ::, and :3:30 is an emoji named "3" hugging "30"

Note:this

/shrug

a://b

HTTPS://SHOUTING.example/LOUD

name: adversarial/delimiter-soup
html:
*a b* c and the mirror **a b** c

***three are literal*** but two nest fine

intraword: 234 and snake_case_names and a * spaced * out

*stars in code* and a literal ` inside doubles and `unclosed

struck ~single~ ~~~triple~~~

*emphasis may not cross a span* boundary

escaped *stars* and ~~tildes~~ stay prose

a hard break
lands here, and this paragraph ends in a lone backslash\

name: adversarial/directive-maze
html: words survive every fence mistake

name: adversarial/fence-fakeout
html:
:::page layout=basic
- not a list
%% not a comment
> not a quote

Four backticks quote three:

```
still inside
```

::: this close line is code; the real closers follow

The next fence never closes - it swallows to EOF and auto-closes there:

these final words are code forever

name: adversarial/list-labyrinth
html:
Shopping list:

  • interrupts the paragraph above

  • one-space floors to sibling

    • three-space floors to nested

  • back to top level

  1. first

  2. all ones - the renderer numbers

  • kind switch splits the list

plain column-0 text restarts everything

  1. numbering restarted here (visible, fixable)

  • item with

    a second paragraph

    fenced code in a bullet

  • -this second dash is content, not a marker

  • a quoted list

  • stays in the quote

    • nested inside both

-not a list, - escaped dash, and three break-alikes:

---

--- x

name: examples/blog-entry
html:
The Great Hat Migration of 2031

Every spring, the hats come down from the north attic. Nobody knows who put them there, and frankly nobody is asking.

What we found

  • Seventeen fedoras, all slightly damp

  • A single, magnificent sombrero

  • Three hats we are legally contractually forbidden to describe

I wrote a little script to catalogue them:

for hat in attic.hats():
    print(hat.vibe, hat.dampness)

The sombrero deserves its own post. For now, here is the webring that started it all:

https://hatenjoyers.example/ring

More soon. :tophat:

name: examples/friendly-message
html:
omg did you SEE the sombrero post :sob: :tophat:

i am crying weeping. we are going to the attic this weekend, no excuses

[the sombrero in question]

(also i fixed the thing you mentioned - it was a missing semicolon, as always)

name: examples/real-blog-house-of-leaves
html:
So, 25 years after it launched, I finally read House of Leaves.

That time I read Homestuck 5 times over 5 consecutive years1:

1I feel like I should write a very long blog post about this sometime.

  • it's like if House of Leaves was awesome instead of horseshit

  • I've never actually read house of leaves it sounds like horseshit though

That time I read Neverending Story:

(side note: this is also why I've been holding off on House of Leaves, which is also in my reading queue now that I've got a color e-reader)

So I've been aware of House of Leaves for a long time, but I wasn't willing to fully tackle it until I had a large format full-color e-reader.

Which, uh: I guess I've had one of those for a little over a year now. So I guess it is time.

I'll even write an article about it.2

2with a bunch of images that I stole from the r/houseofleaves subreddit because they are funny and illustrative.

Reading Patterns

The first 50 pages of House of Leaves took me weeks to read. I'd open it, power through some boring-ass text, fail to see what the deal was, and put it back down.

The last 700 pages of House of Leaves took me 2 days to read. Once I got in to it, it became harder to put down.

This Is Not For You

I bet you'd live to criticize that, wouldn't you, you Critics! But you can't. It's not for you.

This is also, uh, maybe a good time to talk a little bit about how divisive House of Leaves is. Loved by many, hated by many.

Beyond that, though, what does "this book is not for you" mean, at the beginning of the book, in Johnny Truant's font3? Well, it could mean any number of things, each interpretation supported by the text:

3oh, btw, every narrator of this book is written with a different typeface.

  • This is a note from Johnny Truant to himself, pointing out that this book is potentially dangerous to his sanity4.

    4which: it is, good call Johnny

  • This is a note from Johnny to the potential reader, warning them that this book is dangerous to their sanity5.

    5which: it is not, bad call Johnny

  • This indicates Johnny compiled the book for personal reasons, rather than to share it with the world6.

    6Definitely true: Johnny never seems to care about the book's popularity - he is compiling it for his own, personal reasons.

  • Mark Danielewski wants you to know he doesn't care if you like this book, because he wrote it following the death of his own father as a meditation on grieving a parent who was not always kind.

Okay, there we go, we've made it through the first line of the book, only about ten thousand more to go.

Horror Thrives Off of Ambiguity

Horror is better when it goes underexplained: spooky things don't tend to remain spooky under careful examination.

In this 30-minute long7 YouTube video, Hbomberguy makes the point that horror suffered in theaters and thrived on VHS partially because the format itself obscured the fine details:

7by this youtuber's current standards, this is an unbelievably short video

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xbZMqS-fW-8

Longer horror series tend towards separating into episodic horror stories — Black Mirror, Twilight Zone, Goosebumps — and that's partially because horror just works better in shorter form. So in order for longer-form horror to thrive, it has to be, well, incredibly oblique. And boy, does House of Leaves understand the assignment.

Puzzle-Box Writing

In fact, a lot of the time it does this too well: House of Leaves raises a lot of questions and doesn't seem too concerned about answering them, ever.

One of the most prominent questions is that the word "house" is always written in blue, regardless of who's narrating. Why? It can't be a persistent tic of any individual narrator, because then it wouldn't be consistent throughout the whole book. Of course this is never explicitly explained.

This was a major criticism of shows like Lost and the unsatisfying ending of X-Files:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LkoGBOs5ecM

They raise a lot of juicy questions because juicy questions get people engaged, but then never answer them, earning the derogatory term "puzzle-box screenwriting." I'll argue that House of Leaves8 manages to stick these landings better than Sherlock or Lost, because it makes its own refusal to explain itself thematically important.

8and a different "puzzle box" obsession of mine, Homestuck. — Homestuck actually explains almost everything, to full satisfaction, if you bother to close read it. Hussie raised impossibly complicated questions, wrote it live, and had access to a rabid fan community who created impossibly detailed theories; then he quietly read the forums and implemented the fan theories. This creates the impression that he's a brilliant author when he just created compelling questions and crowdsourced the solutions.

The Structure

Talking about the book requires that I give you a brief look at what happens in it. Beware: past this point be spoilers (for a 25-year old book).

  • Johnny Truant, a punk kid mostly interested in drugs and sex, comes into possession of the rambling, mostly insane notes of Zampanó, an elderly blind man who was writing a book.

  • Johnny decides to make it his mission to clean this book up and get it publishable.

  • The actual book is an academic treatise examining "The Navidson Record", a short documentary (imagine "The Blair Witch Project") covering explorations of a very clearly evil house.

  • In a layered double-cross it turns out The Navidson Record and its academic discussion are not real: most references are to books that never existed. The entire book is an invention of Zampanó.

  • Compiling the book is difficult: Johnny slowly loses his mind, withdrawing from society.

  • Within The Navidson Record, Will Navidson moves into a house only to discover it is just a little bigger on the inside than the outside.

  • A freshly discovered door leads to a dark hallway. Down the hallway: another, an endless procession of lightless rooms getting bigger and more abstract the deeper Will explores.

  • An Exploration Team goes in with ropes and lights, and mostly just goes crazy and kills one another.

  • Will's family escapes; Will broods, obsesses, and eventually goes back in.

  • The book ends. Some Zampanó appendices and footnotes follow.

  • The Appendices include letters Johnny saved from his mother, Pelafina, who wrote from the mental institution where she was kept after she attempted to strangle Johnny to death when he was 4, ending with the notice of her suicide.

and that's it, that's the book!

The Book Itself Contorts

The thing a lot of people remember about House of Leaves is that, in the parts within the deepest, darkest parts of the house, the book's text gets wonky, contorting and shifting to force the reader to adjust to its strangeness.

Another detail is the near-constant footnotes9: Johnny Truant, arguably the main character, exists only within the introduction and footnotes.

9nobody else does this

Zampanó Might Be Worse

He apes a lot of conventions from academic writing, including endless navel gazing. He can't form a complete thought without referencing a dozen other works. But of course Zampanó is generating new ideas: his references are entirely fabricated10, as is the entire topic his book covers.

10ChatGPT learned it from Zampanó.

You know, like when folks just read the Wikipedia article and reference all of the things referenced by the Wikipedia article without mentioning that all the work was A Single Wikipedia Page All Along.11

11This was the subject of YET ANOTHER hbomberguy video, I need to find a different guy to reference, why is this one video essayist so frequently relevant?

The Impossible Question of Who Wrote "House of Leaves"

I think one of the core mysteries is who wrote what. Every source of information comes from an unreliable narrator who may or may not even exist. It's a 50 unreliable narrator pile-up!

Johnny could be a fiction created by Zampanó. Zampanó could be a fiction created by Johnny. Pelafina's footnotes introduce a brilliant mad-woman who constantly introduces themes that later show up in the book. The question of who invented what is both central to the mystery and utterly unanswerable.

The Book Is The House, The House is the Book

I'm a sucker for meaningful framing devices. The book, House of Leaves, has a cover that's a little short - it doesn't extend all the way to the end of the pages. It's, uh, bigger on the inside than the outside.

The book describes a labyrinth that demands to be explained, yet contains no answers and engenders obsession and madness from people who demand those answers. Also the book itself demands to be explained, yet contains no answers and engenders obsession and madness from people who demand those answers. The main character tries to fully understand the mysteries of the book and goes mad trying.

Seemingly an endless, infinite, self-referential loop, but actually pretty easily explained if you think about it.

The Actual Answer To The Impossible Question

Mark Danielewski, obviously. Someone had to point these mirrors at one another and then hand it to you like some kinda big fuckin' mystery.

Johnny's Mother's Impossible to Decipher Letter

When Pelafina became increasingly paranoid, she'd layer encoded messages into her messages to her son - messages that, when decoded, felt more real (because of the effort) but were, of course, still nonsense. Then she sends this letter:

It seems pregnant with meaning, overflowing with a code that lies just below the surface — but I checked online, and 25 years later nobody has cracked it. Because there is nothing to crack.

Page 633

This page. This fucking page. I've spent a lot of time on this page. I think that's maybe the point of it. Here, at the nucleus of Pelafina's madness, the riddle is unsolvable. No matter how much effort Johnny or the reader puts into these words, they mean nothing.

However.

The words may not mean much in themselves, but their significance is so enormous that any all-encompassing interpretation of House of Leaves must account for them. They reappear all over the book - in Johnny's poems, in Pelafina's letters, and even in Zampanò's writing.

This book is this page, too: a meaningless labyrinth, meant to be explored but not understood. Like the Codex Seraphinianus it's meant to evoke internal consistency without needing to contain it.

That's Why It Works

I think House of Leaves works because it is thematically and structurally about unsolvable mysteries. It's a rumination on the meaning and draw of unsolvable mysteries.12

12Like, for example, the unsolvable mystery of death, which had just, at time of writing, claimed Mark's father. Grief, too, is a major theme. The house is death. The house is grief.

The book is the house, the house is the book, and both are impossible, unresolvable labyrinths concocted by grieving conmen with unhealthy fixations.

Infohazard

good god damn this book is hard to stop thinking about

I'll be chillin' at work trying to put together Avatar Grembling or whatever the feature of the week is and suddenly I'll just think "but was Johnny real tho". this book is exactly the sort of infohazard it purports to be

Why Is The Internet Obsessed With This Book?

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kMmBFnTfnn0

Just because a book is metanarratively and thematically about being badly written, impenetrable, sexist, frustrating, self-contradictory, meaningless, and the topic of endless circular debate... doesn't... mean it's not badly written, impenetrable, sexist, frustrating, self-contradictory, meaningless, and the topic of endless circular debate.

Something can be fascinating and even self aware without being... y'know... good.13

13you know, like (he gestures around) me

Conclusion

anyhoo, that's a soft recommend from me
